subject: mathematics
prose_summary:
Section 1.1 identifies the magnitude of one lakh through a real-world context — the story of Eshwarappa, a Karnataka farmer who learns that India once had a lakh varieties of rice. The section uses a place-value pattern box (3-digit → 4-digit → 5-digit → 6-digit boundary values) to establish 1,00,000 as the smallest 6-digit number, read as 'One Lakh'. Students are asked to compute how many days in a 100-year lifetime and compare that with one lakh, building intuitive scale. Contextual examples — hairs on a head, a cricket stadium's capacity, rice seed biodiversity — present one lakh alternately as large and small, developing number sense. The section then covers reading and writing numbers in the Indian place value system, including numbers beyond a lakh up to the tens-of-lakhs range, using commas in the 3-2-2 pattern. Students write numbers in words and reconstruct numbers from word descriptions, consolidating place value recall for six and seven-digit numbers.

Section 1.2 reasons through the structure of the base-ten system via the 'Land of Tens' metaphor. Three calculator characters — Thoughtful Thousands (+1000 button), Tedious Tens (+10 button), and Handy Hundreds (+100 button) — ask how many presses are needed to reach specified targets, then introduces Creative Chitti and Systematic Sippy to explore multiple representations of the same number and the connection between minimum button clicks and place-value notation. Students justify why the minimum number of presses equals the digit-sum of the number, and then reason about whether some numbers are reachable by certain calculators but not others. This section develops reasoning about place-value decomposition and then applies that reasoning to compute efficient representations, making both dominant acts clearly sequential.

Section 1.3 identifies and compares the Indian and American/International naming systems for large numbers. It defines crore (1,00,00,000), arab (1,00,00,00,000), million, and billion through a side-by-side table, noting the 3-2-2 Indian comma pattern versus the 3-3-3 American pattern. Students read nine and ten-digit numbers in both systems, convert between word forms and numerals, and compare quantities across systems using inequality symbols. The section applies place value understanding to numbers that now extend to arabs and billions, making both the recall of new number names and their computational application evident.

Section 1.4 explains the distinction between exact and approximate values and justifies when each is appropriate. It introduces rounding up and rounding down through everyday situations (ordering sweets, estimating an item's cost), and develops the concept of 'nearest neighbours' — rounding a number to the nearest thousand, ten thousand, lakh, ten lakh, and crore. Students then apply estimation to compare two children's estimates of sums and differences, reason about whether results exceed or fall below midpoint values, and use a population table of 20 Indian cities (2001 and 2011 data) to practise approximation in context. The section applies rounding and estimation throughout rather than treating them as abstract procedures.

Section 1.5 reasons about and applies multiplication shortcuts. It begins with Roxie's method (multiplying by 5 = dividing by 2 and multiplying by 10) and Estu's method (multiplying by 25 = multiplying by 100 and dividing by 4), both demonstrated as worked examples. Students are asked to justify why these equivalences hold using the meaning of multiplication and division, which is a reasoning act. The 'How Long is the Product?' sub-section discovers patterns in products of numbers with repeated digits and proves, using boundary-case reasoning, that the product of two d-digit numbers is either (2d−1)-digit or 2d-digit. Both the reasoning about why shortcuts work and the pattern-discovery and application exercises make the dual goal sequence clear.

Section 1.6 applies large-number arithmetic to open-ended 'Did You Ever Wonder?' estimation problems. Students compute whether Mumbai's population fits in 1 lakh buses, whether Roxie could travel to the Moon in 10 years at 100 km/day, and whether she could reach the Sun in a lifetime at 1000 km/day. A set of Fascinating Facts problems requires students to compute hidden products and quotients (kīrtanas by Purandaradāsa: 1250 × 380; Earth-Sun distance: 2100 × 70,000; Amazon river discharge: 6400 × 62,500; train journey: 13,95,000 ÷ 150; blue whale weight: 10,50,00,000 ÷ 700; global plastic waste: 52,00,00,00,000 ÷ 130) and read results in both naming systems. A final Figure it Out block (14 questions) consolidates all chapter skills: digit-constrained number construction, letter-counting in number names, digit-rearrangement puzzles, large-number division, bird migration calculations, and coin-stacking estimation. The dominant act is application throughout.

enumerated_worked_examples.description: Roxie evaluates 116 × 5 by rewriting 5 as 10/2, halving 116 to get 58, then multiplying by 10 to obtain 580. Demonstrates multiplying by 5 as dividing by 2 then multiplying by 10.
Estu evaluates 824 × 25 by rewriting 25 as 100/4, dividing 824 by 4 to get 206, then multiplying by 100 to obtain 20,600. Demonstrates multiplying by 25 as multiplying by 100 and dividing by 4.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 1.1 p.3
enumerated_exercises.description: According to the 2011 Census, Chintamani's population was about 75,000. How much less than one lakh is 75,000?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 1.1 p.3
enumerated_exercises.description: Estimated population of Chintamani in 2024 is 1,06,000. How much more than one lakh is 1,06,000?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 1.1 p.3
enumerated_exercises.description: By how much did the population of Chintamani increase from 2011 to 2024?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.1 p.2
enumerated_exercises.description: Choose a number for y. How close to one lakh is the number of days in y years? Also: if a person ate 3 varieties of rice every day, would they taste all lakh varieties in a 100-year lifetime? Standalone ? prompts building intuition about lakh.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 1.2 p.5
enumerated_exercises.description: Thoughtful Thousands (+1000 button): how many presses to show (a) 3,000 (b) 10,000 (c) 53,000 (d) 90,000 (e) 1 lakh (f) ? for 153 presses (g) how many thousands make one lakh?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 1.2 p.5
enumerated_exercises.description: Tedious Tens (+10 button): how many presses to show (a) 500 (b) 780 (c) 1000 (d) 3700 (e) 10,000 (f) 1 lakh (g) ? for 435 presses.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 1.2 p.5
enumerated_exercises.description: Handy Hundreds (+100 button): how many presses to show (a)–(h) given targets; (i) hundreds to make ten thousand; (j) hundreds to make one lakh; (k) evaluate Handy Hundreds' claim about numbers Tedious Tens and Thoughtful Thousands cannot show.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 1.2 p.6
enumerated_exercises.description: Creative Chitti: for each of 8300, 40629, 56354, 66666, 367813, write expressions for at least two different ways to obtain the number through button clicks.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 1.2 p.6
enumerated_exercises.description: Find a different way to get 5072 beyond the two shown, and write an expression for it.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.2 p.7
enumerated_exercises.description: Creative Chitti challenges: (a) make the largest and smallest 3-digit numbers using exactly 30 button presses; (b) make 997 with a different number of clicks than 25.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.2 p.7
enumerated_exercises.description: Systematic Sippy: find how to get (a) 5072 and (b) 8300 using as few button clicks as possible. Write the expression.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 1.2 p.7
enumerated_exercises.description: For 8300, 40629, 56354, 66666, 367813: find the expression giving the smallest number of button clicks.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Math Talk Q2, section 1.2 p.7
enumerated_exercises.description: Do you see any connection between each number and the corresponding smallest number of button clicks?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Math Talk Q3, section 1.2 p.7
enumerated_exercises.description: If you notice, the expressions for least button clicks also give the Indian place value notation of the numbers. Think about why this is so.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.3 p.8
enumerated_exercises.description: How many zeros does a thousand lakh have? How many zeros does a hundred thousand have? Standalone ? prompts on zero-counting for large named numbers.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 1.3 p.9
enumerated_exercises.description: Read six numbers (4050678, 48121620, 20022002, 246813579, 345000543, 1020304050) and write their number names in both the Indian and American systems.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 1.3 p.9
enumerated_exercises.description: Write four numbers in Indian place value notation from word descriptions spanning crores, billions, and millions.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 1.3 p.9
enumerated_exercises.description: Compare and write '<', '>' or '=' for: (a) 30 thousand vs 3 lakhs; (b) 500 lakhs vs 5 million; (c) 800 thousand vs 8 million; (d) 640 crore vs 60 billion.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.4 p.10
enumerated_exercises.description: Think and share situations where it is appropriate to (a) round up, (b) round down, (c) either rounding is okay, (d) exact numbers are needed.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.4 p.11
enumerated_exercises.description: Write the five nearest neighbours (nearest thousand, ten thousand, lakh, ten lakh, crore) for: (a) 3,87,69,957 and (b) 29,05,32,481.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Math Talk, section 1.4 p.11
enumerated_exercises.description: I have a number for which all five nearest neighbours are 5,00,00,000. What could the number be? How many such numbers are there?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 1.4 p.11
enumerated_exercises.description: Estimate 4,63,128 + 4,19,682: evaluate Roxie's and Estu's estimates; determine if sum exceeds 8,50,000; determine if sum exceeds 8,83,128; compute exact value.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 1.4 p.11
enumerated_exercises.description: Estimate 14,63,128 − 4,90,020: evaluate Roxie's and Estu's estimates; determine if difference exceeds 9,50,000; determine if difference exceeds 9,63,128; compute exact value.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 1.4 p.13
enumerated_exercises.description: Populations of Cities: What is your general observation about this data?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 1.4 p.13
enumerated_exercises.description: Populations of Cities: What is an appropriate title for the above table?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 1.4 p.13
enumerated_exercises.description: Populations of Cities: How much is Pune's population in 2011? By approximately how much has it increased compared to 2001?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 1.4 p.13
enumerated_exercises.description: Populations of Cities: Which city's population increased the most between 2001 and 2011?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 1.4 p.13
enumerated_exercises.description: Populations of Cities: Are there cities whose population has almost doubled? Which are they?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 1.4 p.13
enumerated_exercises.description: Populations of Cities: By what number should we multiply Patna's population to get a number close to that of Mumbai?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Math Talk, section 1.5 p.14
enumerated_exercises.description: Using the meaning of multiplication and division, explain why multiplying by 5 is the same as dividing by 2 and multiplying by 10.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 1.5 p.14
Figure it Out Q2, section 1.5 p.14
Figure it Out, section 1.5 p.14
enumerated_exercises.description: Evaluate four sets of pattern multiplications (11×11, 111×111 …; 66×61, 666×661 …; 3×5, 33×35 …; 101×101, 102×102 …) and extend each pattern.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.5 p.15
enumerated_exercises.description: Observe the number of digits in factors and products: is there a connection? Evaluate Roxie's claim that the product of two 2-digit numbers is always 3- or 4-digit. Is there a better way than exhaustive checking?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.5 p.15
enumerated_exercises.description: Can multiplying a 3-digit number with another 3-digit number give a 4-digit number? Can multiplying a 4-digit number with a 2-digit number give a 5-digit number?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.5 p.15
enumerated_exercises.description: Extend the digit-product pattern table: fill in results for 5-digit × 5-digit, 8-digit × 3-digit, and 12-digit × 13-digit.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.6 p.16
enumerated_exercises.description: Fascinating Facts — compute products/quotients to uncover hidden facts: (a) 1250 × 380 = kīrtanas by Purandaradāsa; (b) 2100 × 70,000 = approximate Earth-Sun distance in km; (c) 6400 × 62,500 = Amazon river discharge litres/second; (d) 13,95,000 ÷ 150 = longest single-train journey km; (e) 10,50,00,000 ÷ 700 = blue whale weight kg; (f) 52,00,00,00,000 ÷ 130 = global plastic waste weight in tonnes. Read results in both naming systems.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.6 p.18
enumerated_exercises.description: Could Mumbai's entire population (1 crore 24 lakh) fit into 1 lakh buses (50 people each)? Could it fit into 5000 Titanic ships (2500 passengers each)?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.6 p.19
enumerated_exercises.description: If Roxie travels 100 km/day, can she reach the Moon (3,84,400 km) in 10 years? Find out if you can reach the Sun in a lifetime travelling 1000 km/day.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 1.6 p.19
enumerated_exercises.description: Make necessary assumptions and answer: (a) Can you lift 1 lakh sheets of paper (5 g each)? (b) If 250 babies born per minute, will 1 million be born in a day? (c) Can you count 1 million coins in a day at 1 coin/second?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 1.6 p.19
enumerated_exercises.description: Using digits 0–9 exactly once to create a 10-digit number: (a) write the largest multiple of 5; (b) write the smallest even number.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 1.6 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: Give a 7-digit number name which has the maximum number of letters (knowing 10,30,285 has 43 letters).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 1.6 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: Write a 9-digit number where exchanging any two digits results in a bigger number. How many such numbers exist?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 1.6 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: Strike out 10 digits from the number 12345123451234512345 so that the remaining number is as large as possible.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Try This Q5, section 1.6 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: The words 'zero' and 'one' share letters 'e' and 'o'. How far do you have to count to find two consecutive numbers which do not share an English letter in common?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Try This Q6, section 1.6 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: Write down all numbers 1, 2, 3, …: (a) What is the 1000th digit and at which number? (b) What number contains the millionth digit? (c) When is the digit '5' written for the 5000th time?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q7, section 1.6 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: A calculator has only '+10,000' and '+100' buttons. Write expressions for: (a) 20,800; (b) 92,100; (c) 1,20,500; (d) 65,30,000; (e) 70,25,700.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q8, section 1.6 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: How many lakhs make a billion?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q9, section 1.6 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: Two sets of number cards (1–9). Place cards in boxes to get (a) largest possible sum; (b) smallest possible difference of two resulting numbers (7-digit and 5-digit).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q10, section 1.6 p.21
enumerated_exercises.description: Number cards 4000, 13000, 300, 70000, 150000, 20, 5. Get as close as possible using any operations (each card once) to: (a) 1,10,000; (b) 2,00,000; (c) 5,80,000; (d) 12,45,000; (e) 20,90,800.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q11, section 1.6 p.21
enumerated_exercises.description: How many coins (1 mm thick) should be stacked to match the height of the Statue of Unity (180 m)?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q12, section 1.6 p.21
enumerated_exercises.description: Grey-headed albatrosses cover 900–1000 km/day. One of the longest trips is 12,000 km. How many days to cross the Pacific Ocean approximately?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q13, section 1.6 p.21
enumerated_exercises.description: A bar-tailed godwit flew 13,560 km from Alaska to Australia in about 11 days. Find the approximate distance covered per day and per hour.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q14, section 1.6 p.21
enumerated_exercises.description: Bald eagles fly 4500–6000 m high; Mount Everest is 8850 m; aeroplanes 10,000–12,800 m. How many times bigger are these heights compared to Somu's building (40 m)?
